drag.revealText: Under the violet light, the ink comes back. Edith's hand, small and even. 'March 9th. She moved my chair to face the window again. I moved it back. I like to see the door. She says I am being morbid. I told her I am being seventy-eight. — April 2nd. I telephoned the cemetery at Colchester and asked them to check. They were kind, and confused, and they read me the name on the stone. I sat down on the kitchen floor and I did not cry. — April 30th. She did the dishes tonight, humming. Both plates. The little room's door was shut this morning. It is never shut. I counted the knives after she left the kitchen. I am not being dramatic. I counted them twice. — May 11th. I am writing this in the bath with the tap running. She is better at being Mara than Mara ever was. To whoever reads this: I knew. Put it somewhere in the world that I knew.'

hotspots.examine: Her coat rack. The blue wool coat was her favourite — she wore it into June, said the cold kept her sharp. I should take that one home with me.
hotspots.lookAgain: A coat rack. Four hooks, all worn. A blue wool coat on the third.

hotspots.examine: The mail. I kept meaning to have it stopped. Bills, circulars — eleven weeks of paper from people who don't know yet. I'll write to them all, I promise.
hotspots.lookAgain: A drift of unopened mail. The earliest postmark is eleven weeks old.

hotspots.examine: The hall light. It buzzed when it warmed up, like something thinking. Edith wouldn't have it seen to — she said the flicker kept her company coming in.
hotspots.lookAgain: A light switch. The plate has been wiped clean. The wall around it has not.

hotspots.examine: Oh — the boots are mine. I left them here years ago and she never threw a thing away. Funny, the shapes we leave in each other's houses.
hotspots.lookAgain: Two pairs of shoes, different sizes. Both have fresh mud at the heel.

hotspots.examine: Edith's reading chair. She'd sit here every night with her tea and a library mystery, right where the afternoon came in. She was so at peace in this apartment.

examineTiered.text: The chair again? It's a chair, sweetheart. Old women wear out their furniture — that's all that is. Come away from there.

hotspots.lookAgain: A chair facing the wall, not the window. Deep scratches on the armrests. The floor beneath is worn through the varnish in two parallel tracks.

hotspots.examine: The cushion's gone flat as an envelope. Twenty years of the same dear weight, that's all. Leave the poor chair be — we've looked already.
hotspots.lookAgain: Something rigid beneath the cushion. You lift it. A key ring — five keys.

hotspots.examine: These floors were due refinishing when she moved in. Forty years of other people's furniture — old buildings wear their history. I find it honest.
hotspots.lookAgain: Two parallel gouges, chair to wall and back. Something heavy, dragged many times. They pass beneath the chair cushion.

hotspots.examine: The middle one — the lake house, the summer we turned the boat over. Her hair's plastered flat and she's laughing at me, because I'd sworn I could row. I'd give anything for that day back.

examineTiered.text: Why do you keep counting the frames? It's a wall of a good life, that's all it is. Look at her laughing. Look at her.

hotspots.lookAgain: Six frames, five photographs. The middle frame is empty. Its glass is clean except for four fingerprints.

hotspots.examine: Let's hear them, then. 'Edith, it's the pharmacy—' 'Edith, love, ring me back—' There. Doctors and busybodies. Nothing we need — I hate a machine doing the talking for her.
hotspots.lookAgain: Fourteen messages. Thirteen are from the same number. All of them have been played before — recently.

hotspots.examine: Her telephone. She never would get a mobile. Every Sunday at seven she'd answer on the second ring — some people you can set your week by.
hotspots.lookAgain: A corded telephone. On the emergency card, a new number is written over an old one, in different ink.

hotspots.examine: Her cup, still out on the arm of the chair. Chamomile with one sugar, always. If I close my eyes I can smell it.
hotspots.lookAgain: A cup with a dried ring inside. It smells of nothing.

hotspots.examine: Her mysteries. She'd have the murderer picked by chapter three and read on anyway, just to watch him get caught. She liked knowing how a thing ends.
hotspots.lookAgain: Shelves of paperbacks. One full row is shelved backwards, spines to the wall.

hotspots.examine: Her window. She liked the street better than the television — six floors of other people's lives going by. She could tell you everyone's comings and goings.
hotspots.lookAgain: A sash window behind a net curtain. The curtain's hem is nailed to the sill.

hotspots.examine: Nearly the last box. You've been such a help, you know. Strange work — folding a whole person into cardboard. We're almost through it, you and I.
hotspots.lookAgain: A packing box, half filled. The tape gun resting on it is warm.

exits.lockedText: Not the kitchen yet — I want this room finished first. Play her messages for me, would you? Somebody besides me should hear her friends' voices.

hotspots.examine: Her calendar — look. 'Mara visits,' every week, in her lovely cramped hand. Fifty-two little proofs a year that somebody came. We were close like that, she and I.
hotspots.lookAgain: A wall calendar. 'Mara visits,' weekly. The entries continue six weeks past the date on the memorial card taped to the fridge.

hotspots.examine: I ought to empty the fridge before it all turns. She kept it stocked for guests to the last — you never left Edith's table hungry. I'll bring bags tomorrow.
hotspots.lookAgain: Milk dated two weeks ago. Eggs, half gone. In the bin, a supermarket receipt from Tuesday.

hotspots.examine: One plate, one cup, washed and set to dry. She ate alone every night, poor love. That's the part I can't forgive — all those dinners for one.
hotspots.lookAgain: Two plates. Two cups. Two forks. Still wet.

hotspots.examine: Her pills. Monday to Sunday, morning and evening — she ran that little box like a stationmaster. Managed all of it herself, right to the end.
hotspots.lookAgain: A seven-day organizer. All fourteen lids closed. All fourteen wells full. Dust on the lids.

hotspots.examine: The kettle, older than both of us and twice as dependable. Chamomile every evening, always chamomile. The whole kitchen used to smell of it.
hotspots.lookAgain: The kettle is full and freshly descaled. The chamomile box is empty. The jar of coffee beside it is new.

hotspots.examine: Every kitchen has one — string, batteries, menus for takeaways that closed years ago. Nothing worth your eyes, love. Some drawers are just where a house keeps its rubbish.
hotspots.lookAgain: Beneath the menus, a notebook, warped by water. 'E.' pencilled on the cover. The ink has washed to ghosts.

exits.lockedText: Not yet — stay with me in here a moment. This room was her whole heart. Look properly, love: her calendar, her fridge, her little dishes drying. Then tell me she wasn't looked after.

hotspots.examine: Her violet nightlight — for her eyes, she said. It showed her what the big light couldn't: water marks, smudges on the glass. Everything honest comes out under that little glow.
hotspots.lookAgain: A UV nightlight, plugged in, warm. In its glow the grout shows handprints the overhead light cannot see.

hotspots.examine: Her medicine cabinet. I kept her on top of every bit of it — we'd count the pills out together on Sundays, with the radio on. She never missed a dose on my watch.

examineTiered.text: You keep looking at everything twice. Edith used to do that, near the end. It wore her out, sweetheart. It would wear anyone out.

hotspots.lookAgain: Prescription bottles, unopened, seals intact. The fill dates stop eleven weeks ago.

hotspots.examine: Her sink, her one toothbrush, her lavender soap. She'd scrub her hands like a surgeon before baking. Some habits are the whole person, aren't they.
hotspots.lookAgain: One toothbrush, bone dry. The soap is worn to a sliver and still slick.

hotspots.examine: Fresh towels on the rail. She kept a proper home to the very end — ironed pillowcases, bleached grout. I hope somebody says as much about me one day.
hotspots.lookAgain: Two towels on the rail. One is dry and stiff. The other is damp.

hotspots.examine: Lord, I look tired. Don't look at me, sweetheart — look at the room. Edith always said this mirror made people kinder than they were. Kind glass, she called it.
hotspots.lookAgain: A mirror. It shows the room behind you. The door in the reflection is open wider than the door.

exits.lockedText: Locked, and locked it stays — I couldn't even tell you where her key got to. It's storage, sweetheart, dust and boxes. Nothing in there can tell you anything about her.

hotspots.examine: See? Storage. A guest bed for guests we never had — I don't think a soul has slept in it in twenty years. Sad, really. Don't dwell on it.
hotspots.lookAgain: A made bed. The pillow holds the shape of a head. The sheets smell of detergent — washed this week.

hotspots.examine: Dust sheets, probably, or the old curtains. I'll sort it later — you needn't go through her laundry, of all things.
hotspots.lookAgain: A basket of clean clothes, folded. None of them are Edith's style. The top shirt is inside out.

hotspots.examine: More storage — I did tell you. Boxes and mothballs. You do like to be thorough, don't you, sweetheart.
hotspots.lookAgain: A rail of hangers, half empty. On the floor, a sleeping bag, rolled tight. There is no dust on it.

hotspots.examine: Edith's sewing desk, from before her hands went. Pins and dress patterns and dust. Leave it, love — some things you pack without looking.
hotspots.lookAgain: A desk. In the drawer-well, a Polaroid camera. The film counter shows 1.

hotspots.examine: Take her picture, then — the room, I mean — if it helps you leave it behind. There's one shot left, so frame it kindly. Show me what you see.
hotspots.lookAgain: Through the viewfinder the room is smaller. You press the shutter. The camera whirs and gives up its last photograph, blank, still developing.

beats: There. That's the whole of her, folded and boxed and ready to carry — the tea, the mysteries, the Sunday calls. She was tired, and she was ill, and she went gently, in her own bed, in her own good time.

And I did everything a sister could do. I visited every week — you saw the calendar. I counted out her pills on Sundays. I kept her company right to the end. Nobody could have loved her more carefully than I did.
She was not lonely, and she was not left. I am certain of this one thing — certain as my own name: my sister never spent a single night afraid.
endingA: That's the last of them. Thank you, sweetheart — truly — for helping me say goodbye to her. Leave the key on the mat when you go. I'll only be a minute more.
In the empty apartment, the last photograph finishes developing.

corrupt: She was at peace here. She'd sit with her tea and watch the—
I visited every week. I was there, I was always—
The calendar kept counting after she stopped.
I am her sister. I am Mara. I am—
endingB.mirrorLookAgain: A mirror. A woman stands in Edith's hallway, holding Edith's keys. You wait for a voice to say her name. Nothing comes.
